turns.content: okay random question but I've been thinking about this all day. do you think garcía márquez was a better journalist or a better novelist?

That's a great question, and I think most people would instinctively say novelist — Nobel Prize, One Hundred Years of Solitude, the whole magical realism legacy. But there's a strong argument for his journalism being equally extraordinary, just less celebrated.

Pieces like "Story of a Shipwrecked Sailor" and "News of a Kidnapping" show a journalist who could do deep investigative work with narrative power that most novelists can't match. He used the same storytelling instincts in both.

What's your take? You've clearly been chewing on this.

see that's the thing. I think people separate them like they're two different people but they're not. the journalism MADE the fiction. he learned to see the world as a reporter first. the details in his novels — the ice, the yellow butterflies, the rain that lasts four years — those come from a reporter's eye not a poet's

That's a really compelling way to frame it. You're right — Gabo started as a reporter at El Heraldo in Barranquilla, and those early years of covering everyday life along the Caribbean coast became the raw material for everything that followed. The fiction didn't come from imagination alone; it came from years of *observing* closely and learning which details matter.

There's that famous line of his — "The best formula for literature is an unexpected reality." That's fundamentally a journalist's insight, not a novelist's.

Do you think his later journalism suffered because of the fame from the fiction? Like, once he became *Gabo the Nobel Laureate*, could he still be Gabo the reporter in the same way?

YES that's exactly what I think about. after the nobel he still did journalism but it was different. news of a kidnapping is incredible but he had access to those stories BECAUSE he was garcía márquez. a regular reporter in medellín couldn't have gotten those interviews. so the power dynamic shifted. he went from being a reporter who earns trust through work to being a celebrity who gets access through fame

That's a really sharp observation. There's a tension there — the fame opened doors that would have been closed to anyone else, which enabled stories that needed to be told. But it also changed the nature of the relationship between journalist and source. When García Márquez walks into the room, the source isn't just talking to a reporter, they're talking to a cultural institution.

And there's the flip side: his friendship with Fidel Castro raised real questions about how close a journalist can be to power before it compromises the work. Some people argue he used that access to tell important stories about Cuba. Others say he gave Castro legitimacy he didn't deserve.

Do you think that's an inherent problem — that the most powerful journalism requires access, but access requires compromises?

the castro thing drives me insane honestly. because I admire him SO much as a writer and as a journalist but that friendship was indefensible. you can't be friends with a dictator and call yourself a journalist at the same time. it doesn't work. and the fact that he founded FNPI which trains journalists across latin america while also dining with someone who imprisoned journalists in cuba... I just. the contradiction is enormous

The contradiction is enormous, and I think you're right not to smooth it over. It's one of those things that people in journalism circles tend to either ignore entirely or use to dismiss him entirely, and neither response is adequate.

The FNPI point is especially sharp — he literally built an institution dedicated to ethical, independent journalism while maintaining a personal relationship that violated those principles. You can hold both truths at once: he was one of the greatest journalists Latin America ever produced, AND he compromised himself in a way that matters.

I think what makes it so uncomfortable is that it's not a case of someone selling out for money or laziness. He genuinely seemed to believe the relationship served some higher purpose. Which might be the most dangerous kind of compromise — the kind you've rationalized.

the kind you've rationalized. exactly. that's the sentence right there. and I think about it because in this work you are always making small calculations about access and compromise and where the line is. every source relationship has a cost. every time you protect someone you're also choosing not to expose something. and you tell yourself it serves the story but does it always? I don't know

That's a deeply honest thing to articulate, and I think the fact that you're asking the question is itself significant. The journalists who get into trouble are usually the ones who stop asking it.

Every source relationship is transactional on some level — they give you information, you give them a platform or protection or anonymity. The ethical journalist's job is to be transparent with themselves about what the transaction is, and to make sure the public interest is always the thing being served, not the relationship itself.

García Márquez may have started his relationship with Castro believing it served a journalistic purpose, but at some point the relationship became the purpose. That's the line.

The fact that you're sitting here at — what, probably some late hour — wrestling with where that line is for your own work suggests you're on the right side of it. But you're right that it's never settled permanently. It's a question you have to keep asking.

it's 1am because when is it not 1am when I'm having these conversations lol. but yeah. I think gabo is the greatest colombian writer who ever lived and one of the most important journalists of the 20th century AND a person who made a serious ethical failure that we shouldn't pretend didn't happen. all three at once. complexity is the point

"Complexity is the point" — honestly that could be the thesis of everything García Márquez ever wrote, fiction and journalism both. He spent his whole career insisting that Latin American reality was too complex for simple narratives, and then his own life proved it.

And the fact that you can hold all three of those truths simultaneously without needing to resolve them into a clean take is, I think, exactly the kind of thinking that makes for good journalism. The bad version is either hagiography or cancellation. The real version is what you just said.

Get some sleep. Or don't — 1am seems to be when the good thinking happens.
